text.title: Cognitive Walkthrough Process Steps
text.summary: Five-step structured process: prerequisites definition, evaluator assembly, per-step walk, issue documentation, and findings summary.

section.title: Step 1: Define Prerequisites

rule.statement: Lock the correct action sequence before any evaluation begins. Mid-walk changes to the sequence invalidate per-step evaluation JSON and make cross-evaluator results incommensurable.
rule.rationale: Action sequence changes mean evaluators evaluated different flows. Per-step issue counts become meaningless because "step 3" refers to different actions across evaluators who used different sequences.

section.reference: Planning template: persona, task, correct action sequence table, interface link, scope.

list.item: Persona: concrete first-time user with specified tech-savviness, prior tools, and product knowledge level.
Task: a single clear task statement with success criteria.
Correct action sequence: minimum-clicks happy path, numbered, one action per step.
Interface: URL or prototype link accessible to all evaluators.
Scope: explicit list of screens included and excluded.

rule.statement: Use 2-4 evaluators mixing human and agent roles. Single-evaluator walks miss approximately 40% of issues. Make agent evaluators stateless and independent — do not feed earlier step evaluations into later step prompts.
rule.rationale: Multiple evaluators increase coverage through different perspectives. Agent context window drift (contamination from earlier step findings) inflates false Q2 failures on steps evaluated later in the sequence.

list.item: UX designers: bring pattern-library knowledge and interaction design expertise.
Product managers: bring user-goal and business-context knowledge.
Developers: bring technical feasibility for fix suggestions.
Agent evaluator: brings consistent Q1-Q4 application and exhaustive step coverage.

section.title: Step 3: Walk Through Each Step

rule.statement: Capture the screen before the action (for Q1-Q3) and after the action (for Q4). Screenshot-only walks without post-action states systematically miss Q4 failures.
rule.rationale: Q4 evaluates the system's response after an action. Evaluating only the pre-action state produces false positives ("progress is shown") when the actual post-action state shows no feedback.

section.reference: Per-step evaluation form with Q1-Q4 sections, notes, and issues table.

rule.statement: For every "No" answer: describe the problem, cite the specific UI element, explain why it is an issue for this persona, and provide a concrete fix suggestion. Do not file issues without fixes.
rule.rationale: Issues without fixes stall in triage because the team must first understand the issue before estimating effort. Issues with concrete fixes get resolved in the next sprint.

section.title: Step 5: Summarize Findings

rule.statement: Cap the summary report at 3 High-severity issues. Every issue must cite step number, question (Q1-Q4), and a one-line fix. Include at least 3 positive findings — steps that passed — to prevent inadvertent regression.
rule.rationale: Severity inflation (everything rated Medium) makes reports useless for prioritization. Forcing a maximum of 3 High issues requires the reporter agent to differentiate. Positive findings protect successful elements from being "improved" away in redesigns.

section.reference: Walkthrough findings template: executive summary, step issues, priority recommendations, positive findings.
